phenomenon: Coastal sand transport: longshore drift and tidal sorting
key_measurement: Longshore sediment transport rate at Santa Barbara, California: ~200,000–500,000 m³/year (USACE, measured via dredge records); tidal sorting produces grain-size gradients of 0.1–0.5 φ over 100 m cross-shore distance
institutions: U.S. Geological Survey Coastal and Marine Hazards and Resources Program
U.S. Army Corps of Engineers (USACE) Coastal Hydraulics Laboratory

sources.citation: Komar, P.D. (1998) 'Beach Processes and Sedimentation', 2nd ed., Prentice Hall, Upper Saddle River, NJ
Longshore Sediment Transport, USACE Coastal Engineering Manual EM 1110-2-1100, Part III Ch. 2 (2002)
Masselink, G. & Gehrels, R. (eds.) (2014) 'Coastal Environments and Global Change', Wiley-Blackwell / American Geophysical Union

visualization_compliance: Net longshore transport direction arrow must align with observed dominant wave approach angle for the depicted coastline
Tidal cycle animation must use a realistic period (diurnal ~24.8 hr or semidiurnal ~12.4 hr) — not compressed without explicit time-lapse label
Wave height and period in animation must be within documented range for the depicted location
Grain-size sorting gradient across the surf zone must be directionally accurate (coarser near plunge point, finer offshore)
Do not depict net transport reversals without citing a seasonal data source
Label SCHEMATIC on plan-view longshore drift diagrams; scale bar in meters required
